::: {.cell}

:::


## Introduction to `ggplot2`

::::: columns
::: {.column width="46%"}
![[Artwork by \@allison_horst](https://allisonhorst.com/)](../img_slides/horst_ggplot2_exploratory.png){fig-align="center"}
:::

::: {.column width="54%"}
![[Artwork by \@allison_horst](https://allisonhorst.com/)](../img_slides/ggplot2_masterpiece.png){fig-align="center"}
:::
:::::

## `ggplot2` in tidyverse

::::: columns
::: {.column width="40%"}
![](../img_slides/ggplot2-part-of-tidyverse.png)
:::

::: {.column width="60%"}
-   `ggplot2` is tidyverse's data visualization package

    -   This is one of the main ways to create plots and explore data

 

-   The `gg` in "ggplot2" stands for Grammar of Graphics

 

-   It is inspired by the book **Grammar of Graphics** by Leland Wilkinson

    -   Make graphs/plots by combining independent components
    -   Start with a basic plot then add layers
:::
:::::


::: {.cell}
<style type="text/css">
.reveal code {
  max-height: 100% !important;
}
</style>
:::


## Works best with "tidy" data[^1]

[^1]: Source: R for Data Science. Grolemund and Wickham.

![](../img_slides/tidy-data-frame.png){fig-align="center"}

1.  Each variable must have its own column.

2.  Each observation must have its own row.

3.  Each value must have its own cell.

## Grammar of ggplot2

::::: columns
::: {.column width="60%"}
![](/lessons/img_slides/ggplot_layers.png){fig-align="center" width="1600"}
:::

::: {.column width="40%"}
-   `ggplot2` needs at least the following three to produce a chart:
    -   data, a mapping, and a layer

 

-   For the most part, there are default settings for the other parts:
    -   scales, facets, coordinates, and themes
:::
:::::

## **Data**

-   `ggplot2` uses **data** to construct a plot

-   Works best with tidy data (when every observation is a row and each variable is a column)

-   First step in plotting:

    -   Pass the data to the `ggplot` function, which stores the data to be used later by other parts of the plotting system

## Data

-   For example, if we intend to make a graphic about the `hrs_data` dataset, we would start as follows:


::: {.cell layout-align="center" output-location='column'}

```{.r .cell-code}
ggplot(
  data = hrs_data #<1>
  )
```

::: {.cell-output-display}
![](26_Data_viz_Intro_to_ggplot_files/figure-revealjs/unnamed-chunk-3-1.png){fig-align='center' width=960}
:::
:::


1. We input the data that contains our variables of interest.

## **Mapping**

-   Mappings use the `aes()` function to **map** variables to the different axes on a plot

    -   `aes()` stands for "aesthetics"

## Data + Mapping

-   If we want the `age` and `income` columns to map to the x- and y-coordinates in the plot, we can do that as follows:


::: {.cell layout-align="center" output-location='column'}

```{.r .cell-code  code-line-numbers="3-6"}
ggplot(
  hrs_data, 
  mapping = aes( #<1>
    x = age,  
    y = income
    ) #<1>
  )
```

::: {.cell-output-display}
![](26_Data_viz_Intro_to_ggplot_files/figure-revealjs/unnamed-chunk-4-1.png){fig-align='center' width=960}
:::
:::


1. We use the `aes()` function to specify the mapping of variables to axes. In this case, we are mapping `age` to the x-axis and `income` to the y-axis.

## **Layers**

-   Every layer consists of three important parts:

    -   The **geometry** that determines *how* data are displayed, such as points, lines, or rectangles

    -   The **statistical transformation** that may compute new variables from the data and affect *what* of the data is displayed.

    -   The **position adjustment** that primarily determines *where* a piece of data is being displayed

-   A layer can be constructed using the `geom_*()` and `stat_*()` functions

    -   These functions often determine one of the three parts of a layer, while the other two can still be specified.

## Data + Mapping + Layers

Here is how we can use two layers to display the `age` and `income` columns of the `hrs_data` dataset as points and stack a trend line on top:


::: {.cell layout-align="center" output-location='column'}

```{.r .cell-code}
ggplot(
  hrs_data, 
  mapping = aes(
    x = age, 
    y = income
    )
  ) + 
  geom_point() +  # <1>
  geom_smooth(  # <2> 
    formula = y ~ x, 
    method = "lm"
    )  # <2>
```

::: {.cell-output-display}
![](26_Data_viz_Intro_to_ggplot_files/figure-revealjs/unnamed-chunk-5-1.png){fig-align='center' width=960}
:::
:::


1. To create a scatterplot
2. To fit and overlay a line

## Notice: the following create the same plot

::: columns
::: column
[Naming the input arguments in the `ggplot()` function]{style="color: #5BAFF8;"}

::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
ggplot(
  data = hrs_data, #<1>
  mapping = aes( #<2>
    x = age, 
    y = income
    )
  ) + 
  geom_point()
```
:::

1. We name the input argument for the dataset as `data`
2. We name the input argument for the mapping as `mapping`
:::
::: column
[More concise, no naming input arguments]{style="color: #BF396F;"}

::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
ggplot(
  hrs_data, #<1>
  aes( #<2>
    x = age, 
    y = income
    )
  ) + 
  geom_point()
```
:::

1. Removed input argument name
2. Removed input argument name
:::
::: column
[We can pipe the dataset into `ggplot()`]{style="color: #459B99;"}

::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
hrs_data |> #<1>
  ggplot(
    aes(
      x = age, 
      y = income
      )
    ) + 
  geom_point()
```
:::

1. We use the pipe operator to pass the dataset into `ggplot()`
:::
::: column
[We can also move `aes()` out of `ggplot()`]{style="color: #3070BF;"}

::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
ggplot(hrs_data) + 
  aes( #<1>
    x = age, 
    y = income
  ) + #<1>
  geom_point()
```
:::

1. We move the `aes()` function out of the `ggplot()` function
:::
:::

## We can also make plots with a single variable

-   Data: still `hrs_data`

-   Mapping: using aesthetic to specify only one variable in the x-axis (`age`)

-   Layers: using `geom_histogram()` to show a plot of the counts per `age` 


::: {.cell layout-align="center" output-location='column'}

```{.r .cell-code}
ggplot(
  hrs_data, 
  aes(age) #<1>
  ) +
  geom_histogram() #<2>
```

::: {.cell-output-display}
![](26_Data_viz_Intro_to_ggplot_files/figure-revealjs/unnamed-chunk-10-1.png){fig-align='center' width=960}
:::
:::


1. We specify only one variable in the `aes()` function, which will be mapped to the x-axis by default
2. We use `geom_histogram()` to create a histogram of the counts per `age

## Let's take a second to try this out {visibility="hidden"}

-   Make sure you are working in a Quarto document that has all the libraries loaded

-   Use `glimpse()` to look at the variables in `hrs_data`

-   Choose one of the variables to make a plot for

<!-- -->

-   Go to this site: <https://bookdown.dongzhuoer.com/hadley/ggplot2-book/geom>

    -   Choose one of the "One variable" geoms that would work well for the variable you chose (discrete or continuous options)

-   Make a plot for the variable!


::: {.cell}
::: {.cell-output-display}

```{=html}
<countdown-timer class="countdown" id="timer_91240b6b" minutes="5" seconds="0" update-every="1" tabindex="0" style="right:0;bottom:0;"></countdown-timer>
```

:::
:::


## We can add more to plots!

We can change labels!


::: {.cell layout-align="center" output-location='column'}

```{.r .cell-code  code-line-numbers="6-10"}
ggplot(
  hrs_data, 
  aes(age)
  ) +
  geom_histogram() +
  labs( #<1>
    x = "Age (years)",#<2>
    y = "Frequency", #<3>
    title = "Distribution of Age" #<4>
    ) #<1>
```

::: {.cell-output-display}
![](26_Data_viz_Intro_to_ggplot_files/figure-revealjs/unnamed-chunk-12-1.png){fig-align='center' width=960}
:::
:::


1. We use the `labs()` function to change the labels of the axes and title of the plot
2. We change the x-axis label to "Age (years)"
3. We change the y-axis label to "Frequency"
4. We change the title of the plot to "Distribution of Age"

## Adding more to plots!

Increase (or decrease) text size so we can read it / it fits nicely!


::: {.cell layout-align="center" output-location='column'}

```{.r .cell-code  code-line-numbers="11-15"}
ggplot(
  hrs_data, 
  aes(age)
  ) +
  geom_histogram() +
  labs( 
    x = "Age (years)",
    y = "Frequency",
    title = "Distribution of Age"
    ) +
  theme( #<1>
    axis.text = element_text(size = 28), #<2>
    axis.title = element_text(size = 28), #<3>
    title = element_text(size = 28) #<4>
    ) #<1>
```

::: {.cell-output-display}
![](26_Data_viz_Intro_to_ggplot_files/figure-revealjs/unnamed-chunk-13-1.png){fig-align='center' width=960}
:::
:::


## Notice: common components of `ggplot2`

::: columns
::: column
- Common components of `ggplot2` include: 
  - `ggplot()`: the main function to create a plot
  - `aes()`: the function to specify the mapping of variables to axes
  - `geom_*()` or `stat_*()`: functions to add layers to the plot
  - `labs()`: the function to change labels of axes and title
  - `theme()`: the function to adjust non-data plot elements such as text size,
:::
::: column

 


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
ggplot(
  data = my_data, 
  mapping = aes(x = col1, y = col2)
  ) +  #<1>       
  
  geom_point() +          
  
  labs() +       
  
  theme()                                  
```
:::

:::
:::


## Take a moment {visibility="hidden"}

-   To add labels to your plot and change the text size if you want
-   If you have time, look up help on the `element_text()` function
    -   See if you can tilt your text or change the color
    

::: {.cell}
::: {.cell-output-display}

```{=html}
<countdown-timer class="countdown" id="timer_162c8987" minutes="5" seconds="0" update-every="1" tabindex="0" style="right:0;bottom:0;"></countdown-timer>
```

:::
:::


## Wrap-up

- `ggplot2` is great data visualization tool in R

 

- Grammar of Graphics: data, mapping, layers, scales, facets, coordinates, themes
  - We can add a lot of different components to our plots to make them more informative and visually appealing
  
 
  
- We discussed *some* of the options in `ggplot2` but there are many more to explore!

## Resources on `ggplot`

-   `ggplot2` package website: <https://ggplot2.tidyverse.org/articles/ggplot2.html>
-   Online textbook for `ggplot2`: <https://ggplot2-book.org/>
-   Another online resource for data visualization with `ggplot2`: <https://socviz.co/index.html#preface>

include-after-body:
<script>
  // htmlwidgets need to know to resize themselves when slides are shown/hidden.
  // Fire the "slideenter" event (handled by htmlwidgets.js) when the current
  // slide changes (different for each slide format).
  (function () {
    // dispatch for htmlwidgets
    function fireSlideEnter() {
      const event = window.document.createEvent("Event");
      event.initEvent("slideenter", true, true);
      window.document.dispatchEvent(event);
    }

    function fireSlideChanged(previousSlide, currentSlide) {
      fireSlideEnter();

      // dispatch for shiny
      if (window.jQuery) {
        if (previousSlide) {
          window.jQuery(previousSlide).trigger("hidden");
        }
        if (currentSlide) {
          window.jQuery(currentSlide).trigger("shown");
        }
      }
    }

    // hookup for slidy
    if (window.w3c_slidy) {
      window.w3c_slidy.add_observer(function (slide_num) {
        // slide_num starts at position 1
        fireSlideChanged(null, w3c_slidy.slides[slide_num - 1]);
      });
    }

  })();
</script>
